Thanks for that link!  That was super interesting.   I must say, the whole handling of timeouts/cancellation might be one of the most interesting aspects of Curio right now.  I'm still not sure if it's completely insane or awesome.  There's a fine line between the two perhaps.

One of the main reasons is it makes a socket behave more like a file. You can for example iterate directly over the socket in stream mode.

```
async for line in s:
    print(line)
```

The curio subprocess functions already kill the underlying subprocess on timeout.  In this case, the timeout wasn't occurring because the `timeout_after()` function was being passed `None` (the default timeout on subprocess functions) and it was suppressing the timeout.    This subprocess module was one of the first things done with curio and predates the `timeout_after()` function actually.  So, it's a bit weird.  I'm going to tweak it.

On nested timeouts, I'm pretty sure there is a documentation section about that already.  I know there is a section about what happens when you nest timeouts and how the exceptions get handled.

"Hey look: We are so clever that we can partition our code in
a way none of the parts step on others feet (that's threading).
And even we reached the nirvana where we can cram them all into
a single thread and keep all going. (that's async prog.)"

So by definition the kernel should stay way dealing anything
outside its scope. Its programmers responsibility to
protect it from nonsense.

The available units are not listed in the docs because most of the times it just works. You only need to provide the name of the unit and you get the quantity. That's all!. If the unit name is unknown you will get a nice error message.

But in case you want the list, you can find it [here (https://github.com/hgrecco/pint/blob/master/pint/default_en.txt)

Regarding the docs, I think that "sorely lacking" is a bit too harsh. The docs has a tutorial and explains each feature in a comprehensive way. It is not just a list of API, but shows examples and discuss use cases. This doesn't mean we consider it perfect. There are thing that could be improved (for example adding a link to the `default_en.txt`. And we accept pull requests!

Hi,


your assertion makes sense, we don't check if we're still in the same state before performing the actuall transitions after callbacks have run.

I am unsure about queueing since this is quite complicated to do right. Also if a callback changes state there is no guarantee that it is still allowed when the state transition succeeds. For instance I would expect an onbefore callback to assume it ia in the previous state but if it wants to transition then it has to assume the transition has completed?

We could simply drop the current transition if a callback "escapes" the normal flow. Would this help you? Another possibility is to simply ensure the state did not change when completing the transition and if it did we don't mutate the state anymore.
Need further investigation.

We run our own fork of the wsgidav service on a few Linux servers in our grid setups. Thus in principle we could allow almost any characters on the server side, but enforce some restrictions to prevent side effects when using the files in other contexts.

Comma is allowed in file names on Windows as long as you don't use the old FAT16/32 file systems:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Filename#Comparison_of_filename_limitations
However, to answer your question, a similar issue arises if you (by other means) create files with e.g. colon in the name. Then the Windows file manager refuses all access to the file with an error message. Fair enough really. 
I tried various files with all kind of crazy characters on Win 7 and OSX. Most just work, like those with one or more commas, single quotes and equals signs. Double quote is illegal on both platforms, and we don't support it either, so that isn't a worry in terms of parsing. 
Apart from this comma issue I noticed now that semicolon wreaks havoc from ALL clients in some operations. E.g move 'abc' to 'abc;new' will result in an attempt to rename 'abc' to 'abc'.  Again probably because of interference with query string separators. Maybe it's better to investigate and create a separate issue for that?

I'm not 100% sure if the lack of url-encoding paths is actually a violation of the HTTP protocol but AFAICT the header values should be possible to just split on commas and that's not the case then.
Anyway, I can add a HOTFIX comment and a log warning when it is used - I'll make another pull request then.

```
from __future__ import absolute_import, print_function, division
import unittest
from six.moves import urllib

import vcr

# this is the client library that we're testing
def call_echo(body):
    req = urllib.request.Request('http://gerg.ca/echo.cgi', body)
    return urllib.request.urlopen(req)


class TestEchoClient(unittest.TestCase):
    def setUp(self):
        super(TestEchoClient, self).setUp()
        match_on = ['method', 'scheme', 'host', 'port', 'path', 'query', 'body']
        self.vcr = vcr.VCR(
            record_mode='once',
            match_on=match_on)

    def test_echo_with_body(self):
        body = 'hello world\nhow are you today?'.encode('ascii')
        with self.vcr.use_cassette('echo_with_body.yaml') as cassette:
            response = call_echo(body)

        self.assertEqual(response.code, 200)
        self.assertIn(body, response.read())
```

```python
with IPDB() as ipdb:
    # do some stuff
    …
    …
# here the DB is closed automatically after exit from `with`
```

The issue is that the bit of code that converts the "2.7 months" into a quantity (2.7) and unit (months) is a regex that probably doesn't understand anything other than integers.

The evalModifier code will first pull off "ago" and then send "2.7 months" to be evaluated.

But 2.7 is caught by the RE_NUMBER regex and mangled because it assumes an integer. So yea, the assumption at line 978 or so of __init__.py that the quanity will only ever be an integer probably needs to be fixed.

The RE_NUMBER regex assumes word boundaries:

    (\b(?:{numbers})\b|\d+)

that's the \b part of the above - that would have to change to also include the locale's decimal character (. or ,) but that could also brings in other edge cases.

```python
class Terminal(object):
    """Input buffer moves back onto Terminal."""

    with keystroke_input():  # Renamed from "keystroke_mode", since none of the other mgrs say "mode".

    def keystroke():
        """Keep original, single-char-returning fallback behavior for when tty is in cooked mode, but raise NoKeyboard when timeout is None, lest the program hang forever. If we wanted to be nice, we could even return '' immediately when there is a timeout but no keyboard."""
    
    def _next_char():
    def _char_is_ready():
        """These 2 methods move back onto Terminal."""
```

P.S. A few points illuminating motivations:

* Part of my motivation for moving the input buffer to BufferedKeyboard before was to implicitly flush the input buffer between `with key_mode` invocations. It doesn't, I now see, since the buffering I was looking at is actually just to catch undecodable partial code points. (Ultimately, I predict we'll want to add a flush method to Terminal to avoid unintended buffering between disparate parts of a program: for example, one that makes the user choose from a menu, then does several minutes of processing, then asks for another menu choice. A mistaken key bounce on the initial choice or a cat walking across the keyboard during the processing phase would result in an unintended input on the second menu.)
* Just to clarify, it was always my intent for callers to pass the key() routine around, but, if we're willing to keep the (still kind of strange, I think) single-char fallback behavior of keystroke(), that is one fewer thing to pass around, as you say.

I have some preliminary code at https://github.com/v-chojas/pyodbc/tree/paramarray if you'd like to give it a try. @mkleehammer let me know if you'd like a PR. It has only been tested with SQL Server 2016 and the msodbcsql driver on Linux. To implement the data insertion efficiently, the changes are quite significant so I opted to leave most of the existing code alone and have the new implementation alongside it. Notable differences/improvements are---

1. SQLDescribeParam is used to obtain the correct SQL type and characteristics (e.g. size) of the column, which is then supplied to SQLBindParameter so that the driver sends the correct datatype to the server. This possibly eliminates an additional server-side conversion. In other words, instead of setting both SQL type and C type from Python type, I set the SQL type according to the server's recommendation, the C type from the Python type (using the SQL type as a hint, in some cases), and let ODBC driver do conversion.

2. The entire parameter array is allocated at once and then each row is filled in, converted from Python object to the ODBC C representation. If the "schema" changes (e.g. the cell in a previous row was a PyInt and the current one a PyBytes), then I execute with the rows accumulated thus far, and continue with the current row by detecting and assuming the new schema.

3. As a side-effect, a few bugs I discovered in the existing implementation (execute() in a loop) while testing and comparing were coincidentally fixed:

- Conversion from long integers would fail even if the value was representable in the destination column: e.g. decimal(38,0) can hold values near 2**127 but they failed to convert in the existing implementation. With the optimised implementation, the full precision is usable.

- Used SQL_NUMERIC_STRUCT correctly to avoid a trip through (slow) string conversion. Decimal types now insert with full precision.

- Inserted date/time objects into time(n) columns were missing fractional seconds.

Performance test results:
SQL Server 2016
Microsoft ODBC Driver 13.1 for SQL Server (Linux - CentOS 6.8)
Pythons 2.6.6 and 3.6.1

The sample script for order execution is now present in the standard source distribution

As you point the documentation says:

>The data with the smallest timeframe (and thus the larger number of bars) must be the 1st one to be added to the Cerebro instance

From: http://backtrader.readthedocs.io/en/latest/data-multitimeframe/data-multitimeframe.html#data-multiple-timeframes

And it is for a good reason (although the updated synchronization mechanism should be enough to remove this restriction)  The price is being matched with the `10 minutes` data feed which is a resampled version of the `1 minute` data feed.

That means that `data0` (the *resampled* data) is seeing things late, because it is waiting for a signal (the 1st `1 minute` bar out of the `10 minutes` period) to deliver itself.

In any case and if inserting the larger timeframe still works in your case, you should have issued:

```python
self.buy(data=self.data1)
```

or 

```python
self.buy(data=self.datas[1])
```

A default `self.buy` will buy into the 1st data. The system doesn't know that both belong to the same asset and will make no assumptions about it.

The execution of the order is not *travelling back in time*. The resampled data is delayed because it is resampling. You can see that in that the ´10 minutes` data is not updating whilst the `1 minute` data advances.

In any case I guess (and this is only a guess) that you may be looking for *replaying*. Replaying delivers always and up to date `10 minutes` bar. But you have to take into account that your `next` method will be called several times with the same `len`.

Unfortunately there is no magical one-fits-all solution and that's why the rework of Commission Schemes some weeks ago opened the door for easy subclassing.

Long due post has been published:

  - http://www.backtrader.com/posts/2015-11-20-commission-schemes-subclassing/commission-schemes-subclassing/

Which hopefully should answer the question.

When you work in the shell, there is a delay between the moment where you send the Whois request and the moment where you type `print(who)`. 
This delay do not exist when you call the script itself, you must give time for the request to be processed and an answer to be obtained.
To be clearer, `who` exists as soon as you declare it. So `print(who)` can run as soo as the request is sent... no need to wait for an answer.

BACnet messages are processed in a thread so you have to be careful with those timing issues.

Now, depending on what you need to do with your script, you could use an “inelegant but working” workaround like 
```
import time
import BAC0
bacnet = BAC0.connect()
bacnet.whois()
time.sleep(2)
print(bacnet.devices)
```

Peak normalization is only adding (or removing) a constant gain from all samples, thus changing the volume, but not the dynamic range. In simple math terms, when you do peak normalization, you perform an additive / subtractive operation. For example, your input is:

- minimum =  0
- maximum = 0.8
- dynamic range = 0.8 - 0 = 0.8

To normalize it to a peak level of 1.0, you have to add 0.2, thus:

- new minimum = 0 + 0.2 = 0.2
- new maximum = 0.8 + 0.2 = 1.0
- new dynamic range = 1.0 - 0.2 = 0.8 (same as old dynamic range)

Shown graphically, this is what peak normalization does:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/582444/30522775-142fc578-9bd6-11e7-939b-3cc9e2c0deeb.png)

You take the peak value of the signal and add or substract whatever you need to get to the new target peak value. All samples will be changed accordingly. Note that this curve is of course the volume curve of the signal, not the pure waveform.

What I guess you are thinking about is called compression / expansion, i.e. changing the dynamic range of the signal:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/582444/30522797-4f97278c-9bd6-11e7-8bf9-551fcb724588.png)

In this case you'd *expand* the dynamic range so that the minimum value is still the same, and the maximum value is now higher than the previous maximum. This is *not* what ffmpeg-normalize does and it is *not* what the ffmpeg `volume` filter does. You need the [`compand`](https://ffmpeg.org/ffmpeg-filters.html#Examples-14) filter do to that.

Eh, I see now where the confusion might be. In that chart I am totally oversimplifying, with the y-axis being just "volume". All the normalization happens in the dB realm, and this is what I've been talking about the entire time. You calculate the peak in terms of dBFS and the volume filter adds/subtracts volume in dB. This has nothing to do with what's actually done at a signal level, and of course signal modifications are log-transformed to dB.

I feel like we're not making much progress here. Let me enumerate some of the proposals...

### 1. `on_error` parameter to `consume`

```python
def my_consumer(msg):
    ...
def my_error_callback(exc):
    ...
queue.consume(my_consumer, error_callback=my_error_callback)
```

Pros:
* Backwards compatible
* Consistent with existing callback style
* Simple when people don't care about errors
* Works best when `error_callback` and `my_consumer` don't naturally form an object

Cons:
* Easy to forget `error_callback` - then there's no way to be notified of an error
* Not obvious how to unpack an object into two callbacks, when the two functions do naturally form an object. (An example of such a case would be `QueuingConsumer`.)
    * This makes the common use case quite unnatural - create a `QueuingConsumer`, _pass in the methods individually_, then `yield from` the thing you just passed in.

### 2. `on_error` method on callback object

```python
class MyConsumer:
    def __call__(self, msg):
        ...
    def on_error(self, exc):
        ...
c = MyConsumer()
queue.consume(c)
# then...
yield from c.get()
```

`asynqp` would provide some standard consumer classes like `QueuingConsumer` and `ConsumerAdapter`.

Pros:
* Backwards compatible
* Simple when people don't care about errors
* Works best when message and error actions do form an object (`QueuingConsumer` would be simple to use - just `new` it up and pass it in, rather than passing in the methods separately.)
* Natural to add support for `yield from` or `async for` consuming strategy (eg `QueuingConsumer`)
* Impossible to ignore errors when using a `QueuingConsumer` - they will be thrown into your coroutine.

Cons:
* `__call__` method on consumer object not obvious
    * Variation: don't use `__call__`, use a different name such as `on_msg`.
    * Not backwards compatible; breaks the common case of passing in a function
* Marginally more boilerplate when you want to use separate functions as the callbacks (`queue.consume(ConsumerAdapter(my_callback, my_error_handler)`)
* Marginally more boilerplate when using the `QueuingConsumer`
* Cancelling a consumer is not natural - you `yield from` the thing you pass in, but `cancel` the thing you get back.

### 3. `consume` creates a consumer object when you don't pass in a callback

```python
c = MyConsumer()
queue.consume(c)
# or...
c = queue.consume()
# then...
yield from c.get()
```
Under this proposal, calling `consume` without a callback argument would create and return a `QueuingConsumer`

Pros:
* Backwards compatible
* Minimal boilerplate when you don't want to use a `QueuingConsumer`.
* Could be combined with proposals 1 or 2.
* When _not_ using a callback, impossible to ignore errors. They will always be thrown as exceptions into your coroutine.

Cons:
* Weird, weird inconsistencies. The signature of the `consume` method is not stable
    * This makes this option a non-starter in my opinion

### 4. `consume` always creates a `QueuingConsumer` - no callbacks

This is effectively proposal 3 without the option of passing in a callback.

```python
c = queue.consume()
# then...
yield from c.get()
```

Pros:
* Common case is compelling - especially when you don't need to `cancel` the consumer: `async for msg in queue.consume()`.
* Always impossible to ignore errors - they will be thrown as exceptions into your coroutine.

Cons:
* Backwards incompatible
* Opinionated - no way to customise the behaviour of the consumer. (What do you do if you don't want to use a `QueuingConsumer`?)

### Conclusion

In my opinion, Option 2 strikes the best balance, even though it's a bit strange to `cancel` a different object than the one you `yield from`. Option 4 is a strong contender too, but it has problems with backwards compatibility and has no provisions for customisation.

Do you have any more suggestions, or can you think of any other pros and cons of these options?

`def add_handlers(self, loggers, handler_classes, formatter_class, level):`

Ouch! The only ways I can think of to fix this generally would be to take the DST rules and look for an exact match in all `pytz` timezones. Or create a custom `pytz` timezone class using the rules in the XML. Both solutions are extremely painful.

If you already know what this timezone corresponds to in `pytz`, you could add a custom translation in your own code. Something like this should work:

```python
from exchangelib.winzone import MS_TIMEZONE_TO_PYTZ_MAP

MS_TIMEZONE_TO_PYTZ_MAP['Customized Time Zone'] = 'Europe/Vienna'

# Your code here
```

This assumes that `Customized Time Zone` always points to the same timezone, of course.

I am not sure which of the two following you are trying to do:

1) You want to change the primary key of your model to use an UUID? This is supposed to work, if that's what you are trying to do, I'll have to investigate to find out why it has this strange behavior.

or

2) You want to change the primary key of the translations to use an UUID? I don't really see the use for this as, normally, the primary key of the translations themselves is hidden from the rest of the code (you cannot access it without going through hvad internals). Unless there is a strongly compelling reason to change it, I would suggest to leave it as is. But if that's *really* something you *must* do, you should keeps its name as `id`, because that name is hardcoded is `hvad`'s core. This is a good proposal, there is one thing that we need to be cautious about: MXNet uses asynchronous execution and things can run out of order if it is not tracked by mxnet engine.

Here is an example that things can go wrong.
```python
x = mxnet.nd.zeros((3,4))
x = x + 1
# pass to pytorch or tvm
y = other_framework.nd.fromdlpack(x.asdlpack())
# Y may or maynot be 1
print(y)
```
This is because when we directly call ```x.asdlpack()``` and directly convert the data structure, the execution may not yet finished.

### A Fix
We need to also add flags to asdlpack

```python
x = mxnet.nd.zeros((3,4))
x = x + 1
# pass to pytorch or tvm
x.wait_for_read()
y = other_framework.nd.fromdlpack(x.asdlpack)
# Y may or maynot be 1
print(y)
```
This works for read case, similarly, if we plan to modify the content, we need to do
```python
x = mxnet.nd.zeros((3,4))
x = x + 1
# pass to pytorch or tvm
x.wait_for_write()
y = other_framework.nd.fromdlpack(x.asdlpack)
y = y + 2
# Y should be 3
print(y)
```

### API design
To make sure things won't go wrong because the user does not understand things, I would recommend we use the following API

- NDArray.asdlpack_for_read()
- NDArray.asdlpack_for_write()

@reminisce The probability that the test fails is approximately 0.001 with the 0.01 criterion even if `shuffle` is implemented correctly, assuming the quality of the random numbers are good enough. So it could fail every 1000 runs of the test in average. It looks somewhat high probability. To lower the probability we can increase the number of samples to test uniformity of the shuffling. It is currently 20000. If we increase it as 40000, the failure probability is less than 10^-6 but the running time also increases. In my mahcine the running time increases from 40s to 55s. If this is not acceptable, another way is to relax the criterion as 0.015, which also makes the failure probability as less than 10^-6 but the test becomes less strict. I'm not big on the super call. Inheritance doesn't make sense to me here as BaseOperator isn't meant to be executed. `BaseOperator.execute` is abstract 

At first I was thinking that you could just call a `self.stamp_environ(context)` from execute, but then thought about all the operators in the wild and uneven behavior across operators. I felt like there's something wrong with having to make that call from all operators.

Then I thought that a better approach would be to call `self.stamp_environ(context)` pre-execute from the location where execute is called. Somewhere around here:
https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/models.py#L1289

Operators that want to log more than just the basics could override `stamp_environ`, call super to get the basics and add on to it some more operator specific envvars if/when needed.

For others who have been confused about this, here's a short example of how to use the Facebook batch API with the Python SDK to get ad insights:

```
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helper functions

def generate_batches(iterable, batch_size_limit):
    # This function can be found in examples/batch_utils.py
    batch = []

    for item in iterable:
        if len(batch) == batch_size_limit:
            yield batch
            batch = []
        batch.append(item)

    if len(batch):
        yield batch

def success_callback(response):
    batch_body_responses.append(response.body())

def error_callback(response):
    # Error handling here
    pass

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

batches = []
batch_body_responses = []
api = FacebookAdsApi.init(your_app_id, your_app_secret, your_access_token)

for ad_set_list in generate_batches(ad_sets, batch_limit):
    next_batch = api.new_batch()
    requests = [ad_set.get_insights(pending=True) for ad_set in ad_set_list]
                    
    for req in requests:
        next_batch.add_request(req, success_callback, error_callback)
    batches.append(next_batch)

for batch_request in batches:
    batch_request.execute()
    time.sleep(5)

print batch_body_responses



```

These features are unreleased drafts at this point. Did you configure your libzmq with `--enable-drafts` set? Draft features are disabled by default in current master of libzmq. Since the draft mechanism is *itself* also an unreleased spec subject to change, only pyzmq >= 15.4 even tries to support it. pyzmq should detect the draft API if it was enabled (zmq.DRAFT_API), then `zmq.DISH` should be defined. The additional draft *methods* associated with those sockets are not exposed yet, however.

If DISH is defined, you can create a dish socket with 

```python
zmq.RADIO = 14
zmq.DISH = 15
```

I've had bad experiences every time I try to support unreleased features from libzmq in pyzmq, so I'm planning to wait until closer to release (perhaps after the first beta) before adding these things.

I'v got the error! In my case problem was in mismatching django Timezone and celery Timezone options:

I had this in settings.py:
```
...
TIME_ZONE = 'UTC'
USE_I18N = True
USE_L10N = True
USE_TZ = True
...
...
CELERY_TIMEZONE = 'Europe/Moscow'
```


So I discarded option "CELERY_TIMEZONE = 'Europe/Moscow'" and replaced value for default django timezone setting TIME_ZONE for appropriate timezone value, and it works!
Celery beat stops to spam and now it works as it should to be.

After all my settings.py looks like:
```
...
TIME_ZONE = 'Europe/Moscow'
USE_I18N = True
USE_L10N = True
USE_TZ = True

# CELERY_TIMEZONE = 'Europe/Moscow'
...
```
I hope my case will help someone else

NOTE: Just because it's come up and someone else might want to read this,

The bit about exposing the *MOR ID is bad* thing is my opinion. I personally think that the MOR ID is like a table row identifier of `15` where *virtual machine number 15* is not all that meaningful beyond the local context. So it's not a *good* place to do integration work from. There are *going* to be situations where we're so deep inside what's going on with vCenter that you'll be forced to use that MOR ID. I consider that *unfortunate* and I don't want to necessarily paper over that issue because it's something I feel you need to be mindful of. Whenever you use a MOR ID ... I personally *want* you to have to access a hidden method name because I want you to say to yourself ... *do I really need to do that*?

I tell a story to illustrate this:

Let's say I live in NYC and I buy a parking spot. My neighbor Steve Smith keeps parking in my special paid parking spot. I want the cops to issue him a ticket. Steve Smith is person number `15` in my personal rolodex. If I call up the cops and say, "Person 15 keeps parking in my parking spot." They have no idea who I'm talking about because they don't own a copy of my Rolodex... *nor should they*.

If I refer to Steve Smith by his NYC drivers license number or by his SSN number there's a reasonable chance that a third party can decipher what I'm talking about because these are shared unique details about the individual we're talking about.

With Virtual Machines on vSphere we talk about the `instanceUUID` and the `biosUUID` which would be analogous to the driver's license ID and the SSN number depending on which context we're using. One context is a State-wide identifier the other is Nation-wide. There will be use-cases where no other practical identifier can be found and I consider those *ragged edges* of our API that need to be fixed someday.

This is my *personal* opinion and official VMware guidance may vary.

This is definitely a worthwhile add, so thanks for the PR, but I have one suggestion.

The `requirements.txt` as a standalone file can be useful for a couple reasons.

```
$ git grep requirements.txt
.travis.yml:- pip install -r requirements.txt
setup.py:    os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), 'requirements.txt')
```

We use it in our TravisCI build process. It's also useful for people who've cloned the git repository instead of installing the whole `duo_client` `pip` package. If the requirements are in `setup.py` then you need to install `duo_client` to get them, whereas if we have a separate file you can just `pip install -r requirements.txt`.

I think I see the problem you were running in to when creating the RPM, something like:

```
+ python setup.py build
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "setup.py", line 9, in <module>
    with open(requirements_filename) as fd:
IOError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/tmp/duo_client_python/build/bdist.linux-x86_64/rpm/BUILD/duo_client-3.0/requirements.txt'
error: Bad exit status from /var/tmp/rpm-tmp.53nnYi (%build)
```

We can resolve this by adding `include requirements.txt` in `MANIFEST.in`. Then we can keep the `requirements.txt` file :)

So could you update the PR to add those changes to `MANIFEST.in`, remove the changes from `requirements.txt` and `setup.py`, and squash them in to one commit? Thanks!

Are all logs returned by Selenium as lists of dictionaries? Do these dictionaries contain information about each logged message like the level and the actual message? Do they always have same information?

Assuming the answer to the above questions is yes, I would propose implementing this as follows:

1. Implement a custom class like `LogMessage` that can be populated based on information in the returned dictionaries. Information should be stored to attributes to make it easily accessible in Robot's data like `${message.level}`.

2. Implement separate class like `SeleniumLog` that store `LogMessage` objects. It should be list-like to allow handling messages as lists. Either it could extend the standard `list` or implement `collections.Sequence` abstract  base class.

3. `LogMessage` should have a custom `__str__` to return the message nicely formatted. `SeleniumLog` should also have a custom `__str__` to return messages joined together. 

4. Very importantly, `SeleniumLog` should have custom `__contains__` and `count` to allow using it with `Should (Not) Contain (X Times)`:

       ${messages} =    Get Selenium Log    type=browser
       Should Not Contain    ${messages}    ERROR      # Validate there are no errors
       Should Contain    ${messages}    Some message   # Validate message exists
       Should Contain    ${messages}    INFO: Message  # Validate message with level exists

5. Consider adding support to getting only messages with certain level:

       ${errors} =    Get Selenium Log    filter=ERROR
       Should Be Empty    ${errors}

6. Should also decided how to handle logging the logs. Alternatives are `Get Selenium Log` always logging messages, it having `loglevel=INFO` or `loglevel=NONE` argument to control should they be logged, and adding separate `Log Selenium Log` keyword that gets and logs messages.

Your problem is quite typical one in the web applications. Sometimes it takes awhile for the web application to display the element in the page, sometimes the delay is in milliseconds and sometimes in minutes. Now if one does assert directly, it's very common situation that the assert to fall, sometimes often sometimes rarely, because of the above reason. And with those arguments the `_element_find()` is acting like an assert.

To fix your problem, you should poll that the element is in the page. Depending how your web application is build, looking to the Wait* keywords direction could solve your problems. But it also might be that you may need perform more complicated polling, like refresh the page or performing the navigation again. But this depends how your web application is build and therefore a single solution does not exist to the problem.

But the Selenium2Library private method is working as designed and therefore this is not a problem in the Selenium2Library. Instead it looks like problem how you have using the private method.

As a side note, using private methods is not good practice.  Specially because next major release will refactor those methods: #770

For anyone else coming here: I solved it with this bit of code in my remote library:

from robot.api import logger

def remote_log_message(message,level,html=False):
    print '*{}*  {}'.format(level, message)

logger.write = remote_log_message


This works because most Robot libraries import logger from robot.api - where we replace the definition of write.

@taion By design, `pipenv` focuses almost entirely on the "moving target" security model I described in my LCA talk. Thus, the two intended usage models are as follows:

* Interactive batch updates:

    * A human periodically performs some action (explicit batch update, adding a new dependency, upgrading an explicit dependency)
    * As part of these operations, the lock file and current environment are updated to the latest version of everything that satisfies the expressed constraints in `Pipfile`
    * Over time, either the expressed constraints are updated, and chosen dependencies are switched out, until this way of working becomes painless, or else the developers switch to the second option below 

* Automated selective updates:

    * An automated process keeps watch for new versions of dependencies and submits an issue+PR whenever they become available
    * Humans mainly set up new environments, explicitly add dependencies, and adjust the expresssed constraints on existing dependencies

Now, there are currently CLI design & implementation problems affecting both of those usage models - for interactive batch updates, there are ways for the lock file and the local environment to get out of sync, and for the automated selective updates, there are challenges in implementing the tools that do the selective updates, as well as in enabling the related interactive selective operations to add new dependencies and modify the constraints on existing ones.

But that core design philosophy of "Default to running the latest version of everything unless explicitly told otherwise in `Pipfile`" isn't going to change - we just want to add enough selective update support to better handle the case where most lock file updates are submitted by an update management service, rather than being submitted by humans.

> I'm still not totally convinced about invoking pipenv via cron

Yeah I'm just happy we're kicking ideas around. I'm not married to pipenv having to handle this either, I'm just looking for a clean approach. I do appreciate and respect the imperative to control the scope of pipenv.

For the sake of thinking it through thoroughly, The cron use case might be a good example of what people might end up expecting from *non-interactive* use of pipenv. Dockerfiles might be another area where non-interactive use of pipenv might crop up.

> Out of curiosity, are you sourcing it out of habit or is this actually necessary for your use case

Well, there might be a better way of doing it, but when I run virtualenv-based python projects out of a crontab, I'm sourcing the project's `bin/activate` since that doesn't just give me the right interpreter, it gives me the right path to that virtualenv's package library without having to muck about with python paths. That's necessary if I don't want to install all packages from my virtualenvs in my system library.

> Does just calling the python executable in the virtualenv bin and passing the relevant script as an argument accomplish the same goal?

No unfortunately that doesn't work, and I think for the same reason as the (otherwise really clean) wrapper you suggested won't work either (I just tested to be triple and quadruple sure): because it only specifies interpreter, not the venv's package lib.

> even the alternative case of just using the virtualenv interpreter in your shebang, or something

You already know this so forgive me but I'm just spelling it out in case others read this thread: that just picks the right interpreter again, leaving us again with the problem of pointing it at an alternate location (the venv's location) for packages.

More generally, I'm a little hesitant to hardcode interpreter location in my source code. It's really clean for the shebang to say (as I usually write) `#!/usr/bin/env python` and then to let virtualenv/pipenv handle setting the env right, than to write `#!/path/to/project-hAsHpTH/bin/python`. The `project-hAsHpTH/bin/python` part won't change but the `/path/to/` might very well.

I know it's all a little abstract; if it's easier that I produce a minimal example just say so.

Thanks for your question, 

It is a really good one, and the crux of why it took us about a year to get things right and patch upstream PyPI. We need to take a step back and realize that setup.py has 2 functions:

 - Build the package on the maintainer machine, and release it
 - Build the package on the user machine and install it (whl, or tgz). 

The `requires_python` set a metadata on the tgz/whl so that pip 9+ will not _attempt_ to download if the current Python is Python 2.7 (or 3.0, 3.1...). 

Though if you do _not_ have pip 9+ installed, then:
 1. pip will see the `py3.whl` extension
 2. decide there is no wheels for Py2, thus fallback on the `.tgz`.
 3. It will then unpack the `.tgz` and try to install the package; in which case the `python_requires` is ignored by setuptools. 

Now you have two choice. 
  - Either your `setup.py` is not Python 2 compatible, in which case the install with crash with an underfined error (SyntaxError, TypeError... whatever make setup.py not Python 2 compatible). But better what you _can_ do is fail on purpose with an explicit error message "No, Scikit image is not Python 2 compatible, please update pip, and/or install `<X.Y`" explicitly. 
 - You "succeed" at installing a Python 2 incompatible version, and will fail at runtime. This is suboptimal as then `pip install --upgrade` may break a perfectly working user install, and may only be detected days later. 


Note, `install skimage<X.Y` is also important in the message and not `skimage==X.Y` because you _may_ decide to make point release. We did make the first ipython 6.0 error message say `please install IPython==5.0` and now have a non-negligible amount of user that are not getting any of the `5.x` fixes as they pinned to `5.0` instead of `<6.0`. 

TLDR:
 - don't try to download whl/tgz on recent pip (`requires_python`)
 - if old pip:
   - fail at install time with **informative error message of what to do next** and **leave user Python install in a working state**.

If we made a local copy of the annotations in the constructor, we would eliminate the boilerplate from all five of the methods, and we'd change the `__delitem__` and `__setitem__` methods from using a `try/except AttributeError` to be `if self.__annotations__ is _EMPTY_STORAGE`:
```pycon
In [18]: class Copy(object):
    ...:    
    ...:     def __init__(self, obj):
    ...:         self.obj = obj # For BWC
    ...:         self.__annotations__ = getattr(obj, '__annotations__', _GLOBAL)
```

This results in the best construction times as well as the best access times, no matter whether or not the attribute is present:

| Method                | Python 2.7 | Python 3.5 | PyPy   |
| --------------------- | ---------- | ---------- | ------ |
| init - no attribute   | 1030ns     | 962ns      | 142ns  |
| init - with attribute | 674ns      | 594ns      | 144ns  |
| access                | 293ns      | 155ns      | 26.8ns |

For the case where the attribute is present, the total access times are then:

| Method  | Python 2.7 | Python 3.5 | PyPy  |
| ------- | ---------- | ---------- | ----- |
| getattr (original) | 928ns      | 748ns      | 94ns  |
| direct  (copy) | 967ns      | 749ns      | 170ns |

So they're basically a wash on CPython 2 and 3, and PyPy gets a bit slower. I will note though, that the times under CPython 2 are highly variable, as they are under PyPy (and this kind of micro-benchmarking is notoriously difficult on PyPy).

As a further point, here's a version that uses `try/except` for construction. It slows down construction when the attribute is missing under CPython, but speeds it up for all cases under PyPy:
```pycon
In [35]: class CopyWithExc(object):
    ...:     def __init__(self, obj):
    ...:         self.obj = obj
    ...:         try:
    ...:             self.__annotations__ = obj.__annotations__
    ...:         except AttributeError:
    ...:             pass
```

| Method                        | Python 2.7 | Python 3.5 | PyPy  |
| ----------------------------- | ---------- | ---------- | ----- |
| init - no attribute           | 1690ns     | 1070ns     | 130ns |
| init - with attribute         | 620ns      | 516ns      | 85ns  |
| total access - with attribute | 913ns      | 671ns      | 112ns |

I would say either variant is a win from a code standpoint; which one to choose just depends on how much of a penalty the missing attribute case should get. I think the `getattr` approach is probably a good balance between the two.

I have a design question: there are a few options for potential restructuring to avoid the dereference in the inner loop:

1.  Just use inheritance.
  I.e., derive (non-virtual) subclasses from Table2DImpl for different interpolants.  Probably lots of code duplication to avoid using virtual functions here though.
2.  Templatize both Table2D and Table2DImpl on the interpolant
  Straightforward, but potentially adds unnecessary `template <Interp>` lines in front of every function definition, and unnecessary Table2D<> symbols into the module.
3. Templatize just Table2DImpl
  I think this would require making a Table2DImplBase (since Table2D needs to hold a point to impl), and then deriving template subclasses.  We'd want to move the interpMany functions down into the implementation too to avoid the dereference, so maybe not much gained in terms of reduced symbol proliferation.  The Table2D ctor would retain its signature in this case though, so no need to hack up the pysrc/Table2D file or export anything new into the _galsim python namespace.
4.  Others I haven't thought of...

Thoughts?

No, [the plugin I linked](https://github.com/FortAwesome/wordpress-fontawesome) on GitHub is our prototype. It's not available in the Plugins Directory (and currently, it has a name collision with one of the plugins in there).

A big problem that we've seen often in the WordPress world is that multiple themes and plugins will each attempt to load Font Awesome, but they each try to load their own version and configuration, creating incompatibilities. Also, they are not licensed to load Font Awesome Pro, so you can't legitimately use Pro icons with those plugins.

Our prototype provides back-end mechanisms to allow themes and plugins to register their version and configuration requires, and then our plugin settles all of those requirements down to one version and configuration of Font Awesome that satisfies all registered components—then it loads that one, just one time. It also provides a front-end mechanism (on the settings page in the Dashboard) for the site owner to specify version and configuration options.

So it's a very different kind of plugin from the others.

One of its features is to make it easy to configure support for pseudo elements, even in the SVG case.

@jgeewax It seems your just concerned with the fact that the method lives in each sub-package?

We discussed the idea of a library-wide default project with you at a standup meeting and you signed off on it. (@tseaver [noted this][1] immediately after.)

The method is only defined in one place `gcloud._helpers` but it is imported as a public method (i.e. no `import ... as` rename) in both `storage.__init__` and `pubsub.__init__`.

This is so that we can call it from `set_defaults()` in each of those packages.

----

1.  An easy (partial) way to address your concern will just be

    ```python
    from gcloud._helpers import set_default_project as _set_default_project 
    ```

2.  A remaining issue will be "confusion" if someone does something explicit / declarative like

    ```python
    from gcloud import pubsub
    from gcloud import storage
    ...
    pubsub.set_defaults(connection=conn2, project='proj2')
    storage.set_defaults(connection=conn1, project='proj1')
    ```

    because the value `'proj1'` will become the global (not just `pubsub` only) default. A "wrong global" would not be that big an issue for `storage` since the project is seldom used. It would be a huge issue for `pubsub` since a project is sent in every request.

1.  We could address the above point by either removing `project=` from `set_defaults` or by making the default project local to each sub-package.

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/pull/748#issuecomment-85155384

Hello,
One of the challenges of maintaining a large open source project is that sometimes, you can bite off more than you can chew. As the lead maintainer of `google-cloud-python`, I can definitely say that I have let the issues here pile up.

As part of trying to get things under control (as well as to empower us to provide better customer service in the future), I am declaring a "bankruptcy" of sorts on many of the old issues, especially those likely to have been addressed or made obsolete by more recent updates.

My goal is to close stale issues whose relevance or solution is no longer immediately evident, and which appear to be of lower importance. I believe in good faith that this is one of those issues, but I am scanning quickly and may occasionally be wrong. If this is an issue of high importance, please comment here and we will reconsider. The [TABLE_DATE_RANGE](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/legacy-sql#table-date-range) is a function from legacy SQL. The equivalent for standard SQL is to use [wildcard tables](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/wildcard-tables).

Unfortunately I don't think you can use query parameters to specify a wildcard table.

For this query, it looks like if you [convert these tables to partitioned tables](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/creating-partitioned-tables#converting_dated_tables_into_a_partitioned_table) using the `bq partition` command. Then you could use query parameters with to [query partitions](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/querying-partitioned-tables) using the `_PARTITIONTIME` pseudo-column.

@phobologic My general thoughts:

* PRs are more of an issue due to the need for guidance on the overall architecture. We need documentation on things such as
  * How do you validate parameters
  * When should you create a new validator
  * How are templates tested
  * What's the basic structure of a resource
* On that note overall documentation could use a face lift
* Release cycles I'd probably split into 2 week and 1 month. When AWS is pushing a lot of CloudFormation changes out we should do a 2 week release. If things cool down a 1 month release would be fine.
* Instead of attaching a release to specific issues, I think it should be whatever is doable within reason. AWS batch pull request for example looks like it's going to take a lot more time than expected, and I don't want that to hold a release up.
* We really need to play catch up. Have a bug hunting day or something where we can go through existing pull requests and just start knocking stuff out. I think it would be fine at this point to close issues/pull requests that aren't getting traction with the expectation that they will be re-opened if it's truly a cause that needs addressing.

Is this not feature creep? It is a real stretch to consider this tabular data IMHO, and there is no advantage to using tabulator for this type of data.

If we do want to expand the concept of tabulator to this level, then is this the correct abstraction for HTML? Unlike text files, where we have decades of convention splitting on lines, doing the same for HTML is almost meaningless. 

An HTML parser in the sprit of tabulator would likely view the document as a tree, with top-level branches of `head` and `body`, no? Not so different, conceptually, to our streaming JSON parser based on [ijson](https://github.com/frictionlessdata/tabulator-py/blob/master/setup.py#L25).

I'm not against this if you two are for it, but reading this, it really feels like a "feature" that does not need to be implemented in tabulator in order to support these data sources in Data Package Pipelines.

Looking at the history of `requirements.txt` the exact version requirement was added in 17a135c5d5b7ac1946e6f6959e35c4e711d0007e when it was changed from `lxml` to `lxml==3.6.1` with the commit message, "upgrade lxml." Recently, during development of pyiso 3.20 we bumped it to 3.6.4 in 5c23f6fe29bb3ca6a3b5b8f725c068b97af92c3f to fix a bug.

I think it's a good idea to be less sensitive to versions in our requirements; certainly we should allow users to have a more recent version of a library. I do know off the top of my head that 3.6.4 is a minimum. If you'd like to change `requirements.txt` and `setup.py` to `lxml>=3.6.4` and run the unit tests and integration tests you could see if that breaks anything. If everything passes, open a pull request.

All that said, since you're mentioning lxml 3.6.1 and not lxml 3.6.4, I assume that means you're installing pyiso 3.19 package from PyPI and installing using `pip` or something similar. Typically, when working on a project, it is useful to use virtual environments to isolate one project's dependency version requirements from another. See https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/venv.html for details. Are you using virtual environments in this way, and we're just not playing nice with some other dependency in your project which requires a more recent `lxml` version?

After doing a quick search of hosted CLI usage history, it looks like `server-remove` by hostname is the most common way that command is used.
So I was utterly wrong.

It may be worth doing this. Unfortunately, I can't think of any particularly clean way of exposing that in the API. It seems to conflict a little bit with the REST model.

We can do the current `server-remove -p` behavior with pretty low effort by just doing a list, check for full URI matches, check for `hostname:port` matches, and then finally check for `hostname` matches.
There will be no URI-parsing necessary, as the API stores these values in separate fields.

First pass on this is to track the throughputs and request a header count proportionally to each peer. So if peer A has throughput 1 block bodies per second and peer B does 3 block bodies per second, and we need 8 blocks, we would ask for 2 from peer A and 6 from peer B.

Second pass would probably be to add some kind of outlier cutoff mechanism, once we have collected enough throughput data.

I tried to move this forward and started refactoring the plugin API so that:

1. Plugins are initialized by the plugin manager
2. Plugins get a `PluginContext` injected which serves as an API for plugins to perform certain things including shutting down trinity

However, I hit a road block again. Naively, I first tried to just do `sys.exit(exit_code)` and thought this would propagate and eventually cause a clean shutdown similar to hitting `CTRL + C` on a running Trinity node.

However, that doesn't work and I *think* what we really need is to built the plugin architecture to properly spread across both processes. We currently already have two instances of the `PluginManager`, one in the main process and one in the networking process but that's more a hack than a solution. I guess we really need to distinct parts that are connected so they can talk because then, the `PluginManager` from the networking process (or any non-main process really) could just talk to the other part that is sitting in the main process and tell it to shut the process down. And then, the part sitting in main would be responsible to shut everything down as it's the highest process in the hierarchy and knows about the subprocesses.

I've sincerely thought about it, but I'm going to say no. I don't think it's within PyDelphin's range of responsibilities to perform shell expansion. I considered expanding only path arguments (e.g. for the ACE executable, grammar, --pcfg, etc. paths in the ace interface, itsdb profile paths, file arguments for MRS codecs, etc..), but then users might expect variable expansion in other arguments (e.g. `maxchartmegabytes="$MAXCHART"`), and it would be difficult to know when I should or shouldn't expand things.

Also, if you're using PyDelphin as a library, it's not hard to do this:

```python
from os.path import expanduser, expandvars
grm = expanduser(expandvars(grammar_path))
ace.parse(grm, sentence)
```

> I can't agree, @ukoethe, with regard to builds strings.

That's very reasonable as long as the desired functionality is achieved in a different way. But then the build string should be eliminated from version comparison entirely, because the code at  `conda/resolve.py`, lines 388 and 396 is very suspicious and contradicts the documentation (http://conda.pydata.org/docs/spec.html#package-metadata says that the build string is not used). 

I'm also not sure about the future role of the build number (technically a part of the build string). Are higher build numbers simply shadowing lower ones, or is the rule more complex? For example, if build 1 defines a feature, but build 2 doesn't, which one will version resolution consider if the feature is being tracked? If build numbers are to remain a part of the build string and continue to take part in version resolution (which I think will be the case), there should at least be an unambiguous syntax to indetify the build number in the build string, and improved documentation.  

> plugin architecture for version ordering

This is a good idea, although I'm not sure if a full fledged plugin architecture is necessary. Probably it will be sufficient to provide a small and fixed set of schemes to pick from. Plugins always imply the possibility that a users doesn't have the plugin, which worsens the installation experience.

If anyone is looking for a pragmatic and somewhat gritty way of doing this, here's what I do.

If you're loading config like this:

    config = ConfigObj(config_filename)

but would like a config file like this:

```
api_key = ljalfq9u3fi3jljfkaf
```

to be:

```
api_key = $API_KEY
```

change the Python loading code to:

    config = ConfigObj(substitute_env(config_filename))

And add this function:

```
import io
import os
import re

def substitute_env(filename):
    """Reads filename, substitutes environment variables and returns a file-like
     object of the result.

    Substitution maps text like "$FOO" for the environment variable "FOO".
    """

    def lookup(match):
        """Replaces a match like $FOO with the env var FOO.
        """
        key = match.group(2)
        if key not in os.environ:
            raise UsageError(f"Config env var '{key}' not set")
        return os.environ.get(key)

    pattern = re.compile(r'(\$(\w+))')
    with open(filename, 'r') as src:
        content = src.read()
        replaced = pattern.sub(lookup, content)

    return io.StringIO(replaced)
```

close() "only" closes the high-level consumer functionality (namely leaving the group, committing offsets, etc), but the underlying librdkafka consumer handle remains functional for legacy consumer and basic operations (such as metadata requests!).

Now, there's probably no real use to close the consumer and then do something else on the same handle, so calling rd_kafka_destroy() from close() in the Python API sounds like a good idea.

@arahut All of those arguments are already the defaults, so those wouldn't do anything.

What is your desired output? You want it to throw a `ValueError` because the day and month are not specified?

What does your data set look like? If you always have something of the form '--/--/1963', and sometimes those dashes are replaced with dates (e.g. '01/05/1963') but sometimes not, and you want it to throw a `ValueError` if no month or day is specified, the best way to do that is probably to use the built-in method `datetime.strptime` (since you already know the format of the date):

```python
>>> from datetime import datetime as dt
>>> dt.strptime('04/02/1963', '%m/%d/%Y')
datetime.datetime(1963, 4, 2, 0, 0)
>>> dt.strptime('--/--/1963', '%m/%d/%Y')
    323     if not found:
    324         raise ValueError("time data %r does not match format %r" %
--> 325                          (data_string, format))
    326     if len(data_string) != found.end():
    327         raise ValueError("unconverted data remains: %s" %

ValueError: time data '--/--/1963' does not match format '%m/%d/%Y'
```

That said, I think that `'--/--/1963'` *should* be throwing a `ValueError`, and the only reason it's not is that '-' happens to be a separator. We might consider tweaking the parser such that sequential separators like that need to be explicitly enabled. For now, if you don't know that the format is exactly '%m/%d/%Y', you can try subclassing `dateutil.parserinfo` and removing '-' from the `JUMP` parameter - this will prevent you from parsing dates like `'5-6-1982'`, but it will also cause dateutil to throw a `ValueError` on the provided date:

```python
>>> import dateutil.parser
>>> class no_dash_parserinfo(parser.parserinfo):
>>>    JUMP = [j for j in parser.parserinfo.JUMP if j != '-']

>>> parser.parse('--/--/1963')
datetime.datetime(1963, 9, 8, 0, 0)

>>> try:
>>>    parser.parse('--/--/1963', parserinfo=no_dash_parserinfo())
>>> except ValueError:
>>>    print('No date!')
No date!
```

@pferreir As I said above, `relativedelta` is not inherently positive or negative or larger or smaller than another `relativedelta`. Consider:

```python
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from dateutil.relativedelta import relativedelta

TD_ZERO = timedelta(0)

def diff_is_negative(dt, delta):
    return (dt + delta) - dt < TD_ZERO

rd = relativedelta(month=3)
print(diff_is_negative(datetime(2016, 4, 1), rd))    # True
print(diff_is_negative(datetime(2016, 2, 1), rd))    # False
```

I don't think there will be any intuitive semantics for comparing `relativedelta` objects *in general* that allows them to be meaningfully categorized as bigger or smaller than one another. There are some subsets of `relativedelta` for which comparison is meaningful (e.g. `relativedelta(months=1)` is unambiguously smaller than `relativedelta(months=2)`), but there are a lot of edge cases (e.g. `relativedelta(months=1)` vs. `relativedelta(days=30)`) where the difference in magnitude depends on the context.

Presumably if you're looking for a way to compare `relativedelta` objects, you have some specific semantics in mind. Likely those semantics won't generalize and so shouldn't be implemented in `dateutil`, but there's nothing stopping you from subclassing `relativedelta` to implement your own comparison semantics, e.g.:

```python
from dateutil.relativedelta import relativedelta

class RelativeDeltaMonthComparisons(relativedelta):
    """ A comparable version of `relativedelta` that compares less than based on month offset """
    def __lt__(self, other):
        return self.months < other.months
```

And in the end, I think that it would be great to have this option available in `Meta` too, but still overridable at instanciation and/or at load time. That would give a lot of flexibility, and thus things like this:

```python
from marshmallow import ALLOW, RAISE
from marshmallow import Schema as BaseSchema


# define a default class because in our application
# we want to raise an error by default
class Schema(BaseSchema):
    class Meta:
        unknown = RAISE


# define some schemas
class FooSchema(Schema):
    ...

def some_context(data, ...):
    schema = FooSchema()
    if ...:
        # oh wait, for this schema in that particular
        # context, we want to allow unknown keys
        schema.load(data, unknown=ALLOW)
```

@JianGoForIt As a long as you use the same smoothing parameter, as far as I'm aware all of them use exponential moving average and I use smoothing for the following variables:

- h_min
- h_max 
- grad_f (for variance)
- grad_f^2 (for variance)
- ||grad_f||^2 (for distance to optimum)
- ||grad_f|| (for distance to optimum, note that at time `t` this a `sqrt` of the above but I do smoothing average of the `sqrt` rather than the `sqrt`of the above moving average)
- moving average of the ratio of the moving averages for ||grad_f|| and ||grad_f||^2
- alpha
-mu

The gist have a `LSTMstep` layer which basically has the step function from `LSTMLayer` as `get_output_for`. The second layer is a RecurrentContainerLayer. 

It has 3 new input arguments

   1. step: Network for calculating single time step forward pass 
   2. step_input: Input layer to step network
   3. recurrent_connections: Dictionary which maps recurrent outpus to their input layer.
 
In `RecurrentContainerLayer` i'm not sure how handle initial states properly. For now i just use a method that returns the inital state for the LSTMstep layer, I'm not sure that this solution would work for arbitrary step network structures. Other solutions are welcome!

What was the thinking behind switching to MPL? Usually such choice are made with some reasoning behind them so we should check what that was.

As I understand it MPL does not stop you from selling a product based on `nteract` or even keeping your secret sauce secret. Doing the legal legwork seems like a small price to pay (both in terms of $$ and effort) in exchange for a high quality product like `nteract`. `nteract` receives support from [two companies](https://github.com/nteract/nteract#support) who have presumably found a way to give back under the current model and further their commercial goals. Another one supports the project by allowing staff to use work time.

The [0MQ guide](http://zguide.zeromq.org/page:all#toc145) ([it is about the community](http://zguide.zeromq.org/page:all#Care-and-Feeding)) contains some reasoning behind why a license that allows remixing is a good choice.

The question of how to make your open-source community sustainable has been getting a lot of attention lately, and has been a struggle for a long time.

Given all this I think a more detailed motivation of why this change would make the community/project better is needed. If only to avoid the slight aftertaste of "please make this easier to use in a commercial setting where we don't wish to contribute back".

`selectors.bug_is_testable` is focused on whether or not an issue has been fixed yet, not on whether an issue has been obsoleted by a new version of Pulp. That behaviour is a reflection of the Pulp issue tracker itself. The issue tracker contains information stating in which version of Pulp an issue was first noticed and in which version of Pulp an issue was fixed, but the issue tracker doesn't track when an issue has been obsoleted. That makes me wary of using `selectors.bug_is_testable`.

We could place Pulp 2 and 3 tests in separate packages. For example, one could:

1. Create package `pulp_smash.tests.pulp2`, and move all existing tests into it.
2. Modify `pulp_smash.tests.pulp2.*.utils.set_up_module`. Let the set up functions `raise unittest.SkipTest` if Pulp 3 is under test.
3. Create package `pulp_smash.tests.pulp3`.
4. As a first test, create module `pulp_smash.tests.pulp3.platform.api.test_login`, and import `pulp_smash.tests.pulp2.platform.api_v2.test_login.LoginTestCase`.

This would:

* Allow us to re-use individual test cases.
* Allow some of us to continue using Pulp Smash in the same way: `python -m unittest discover pulp_smash.tests`.
* Ensure that Pulp 3 tests never leak into the Pulp 2 test suite.

Of course, that's a drastic step. We *already* let Pulp tests run on inappropriate versions of Pulp. Tests written today regularly don't include safeguards that ensure that they're skipped on Pulp 2.11 or earlier. And if the majority of tests continue working with Pulp 3, then having to import numerous Pulp 2 tests into the Pulp 3 namespace will be a giant PITA, *especially* in cases where `setUpModule` and the like are used.

I don't think we can make a good decision about how to approach this until we have Pulp 3 builds ready to test.

Yeah, we decided in PR ( https://github.com/pyFFTW/pyFFTW/pull/154 ) to keep Dask as an optional dependency. So the warning is not surprising. As you note, not much we can do about it.

FWIW with ReadTheDocs we make sure to include Dask in PR ( https://github.com/pyFFTW/pyFFTW/pull/161 ). So it should be included and documented. In fact, I can find the Dask interface in the [docs]( http://jakirkhampyfftw.readthedocs.io/en/config_rtd/pyfftw/interfaces/dask_fft.html ) and it looks correct. Just this listing was incorrect by not referencing it.

**Side Note:** The Dask interface was designed to follow the SciPy interface pretty closely in terms of being optional. IOW importing `pyfftw.interfaces` will try to import `dask` and populate the `dask_fft` interface. However, if the import of `dask` doesn't work or the version of `dask` is too old, then we clean up all traces of `dask` and the associated `dask_fft` interface. So importing `pyfftw.interfaces` will always work, but it may just not include certain interfaces if the required dependencies and versions are missing.

> Furthermore, there is a sort of combinatorial explosion happening with all of these different macro combinations :).

Indeed.  We could probably simplify it to just one macro (always require the python name, and take the purity as a true/false value).  Returning virtual method values by reference is probably not common enough that it's necessary to provide 4 different macros.

> When the user wants a reference to a primitive type (int, string, complex, etc.), a SFINAEd version of cast for reference return values is called, which has a static local variable for temporary storage.

Perhaps I'm misunderstanding, but I'd be a bit worried about the implications of just one variable per type: that would mean that any primitive type reference cast would be essentially invalidating all references of the same type across all objects.  That seems like an increase in magnitude (or two) of the re-entrant issue.

This is usually what [Return Value Policies](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/advanced/functions.html#return-value-policies) are for, and if you want to return an unowned pointer from a function you would specify `return_value_policy::reference`. But this won't work for a constructor/`__init__`, so you need a helper function to do the construction and return the pointer with the appropriate policy:
```c++
py::class_<Bell>(m, "Bell"); // no init

m.def("make_bell", []() { return new Bell; }, return_value_policy::reference);
```
Note that `make_bell` can easily be named `Bell` to mimic the original syntax in Python:
```c++
py::class_<Bell>(m, "BellClass"); // no init

m.def("Bell", []() { return new Bell; }, return_value_policy::reference);
```

Alternatively, if you really need to keep the constructor, you could use [`py::nodelete`](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/advanced/classes.html#non-public-destructors) to make sure Python never deletes any objects of a certain type. Beware: this would be like applying `return_value_policy::reference` to any function which returns `Bell` in any form, even if it's returned by value instead of by pointer. The phrase "running with scissors" comes to mind.

It seems like the reason for this bug is similar to that for the bug #710581.

When on a DataLinkConnection object close() is called and state is ESTABLISHED, it sends a DISC-PDU, changes the state to DISCONNECT and waits for an answer in the recv() method before calling super's close().

If remote side terminates connection inbetween or if link is broken, state is DISCONNECT when close() is called by LLC. So, no DISC is sent, but super's close() is called directly. This leads to clearing the receiving buffer and notifying all the waiting threads. Consequence is that the previously started closing thread wakes up and tries to read from empty queue.

New methods for handling TRiP98 are created in https://github.com/pytrip/pytrip/blob/7f660f538f04562857a9308cb48c149543fc733b/pytrip/util.py#L38

This is based on my experience with TRiP98 way of reading and saving the files.

There are two classes: one called TRiP98FilePath which is responsible for dealing with TRiP98-like paths and TRiP98FileLocator which is responsible for locating these files in a filesystem.
First one (TRiP98FilePath) can guess the name of datafile is user provides path to headerfile (with or without extension) and deal with all nasty naming schema.

Second one (TRiP98FileLocator) loops over several candidates in filesystem to load a file (i.e. user wants file called "pat001", it the looks for: "pat001.hed", "pat001.HED", "pat001.hed.gz" etc...).

I've also introduced loading files with different name for header and data file: 
```
import pytrip as pt
d = pt.DosCube()
d.read(('pat001.hed','pat002.dos'))
``` 

There is also a lot of refactoring in the cube.py, mainly grouping of the methods. Their order was completely random before.

Please let me know if this goes good direction.

~I've been looking into this the last couple of hours. Looks like it's a lot harder than it seems.~

~`select_related` fetches from multiple tables in a single query, and this is constructed [here](https://github.com/django/django/blob/b9f7dce84b7ab5e198129030eae6c1a4aec83d24/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py#L177). This is then constructed into instances using `from_db` [here](https://github.com/django/django/blob/b9f7dce84b7ab5e198129030eae6c1a4aec83d24/django/db/models/query.py#L1616).~

A hackier approach may work, since is that it's possible to do a weird `select_related` call that fetches subchildren, so it's also possible to produce a hacky workaround:

```python
qs = PlainModel.objects.select_related('relation__childmodel')

real_instances = []
for obj in qs:
    try:
        obj.relation = obj.relation.childmodel
    except AttributeError:
        pass
    # You can also try other subclasses, just add them to the select_related() call above.
```

Note that this raises `AttributeError`: it doesn't try to query the DB, is just fails an attribute lookup (which is **a lot** cheaper!).

You can also specify nested lookups too, suppose that only `childA` has an `address`, and `childB` has a `phone`: 

```python
orders = Order.objects.select_related('child__childA__address', 'child__childB__phone')
for obj in qs:
    try:
        obj.child = obj.child.childA
    except AttributeError:
        pass
    try:
        obj.child = obj.child.childB.phone
    except AttributeError:
        pass

obj[0].child.phone  # No lookup (assuming this particular child was class ChildB
```

It's simple to write a reusable function to wrap around this, so we end up with a `select_related` function that takes a specific queryset, but I don't know how to patch this into existing querysets due to the change of chained calls.

If somebody can come up with changes for a custom `QuerySet` class to inject this behaviour, the last bit (the try/except part), this approach would be perfect (albeit, hacky underneath). (Classes which related to this model would have to use a custom `QuerySet` provided by us, but that hardly seems like an issue).

```python
unnormalize = T.Normalize((-mean).tolist(), (1.0 / std).tolist())
```

Those files are now being developed as part of Apache Arrow, and are at https://github.com/apache/arrow/tree/master/cpp/src/plasma.

If you built Ray from source, they are under `ray/src/thirdparty/arrow/cpp/src/plasma`.

The executable you're referring to is placed in `ray/python/ray/core/src/plasma/`. But it is moved there from `ray/src/thirdparty/arrow/cpp/build/release/`.

The plasma store uses `mmap` to create objects in shared memory and clients of the plasma store use mmap to map those objects into their own address spaces.

The plasma manager is a separate process which is used to transfer objects between plasma stores on different machines (there is one plasma manager on each machine). The plasma manager is a client of the plasma store on the same machine as it, so it uses `mmap` to read/write directly from/to shared memory when transferring objects from/to the plasma store on the same machine.

I've got the trouble: we are testing a pytest plugin (i.e. `pytest-trio`) with pytest, using another pytest plugin (`pytest-cov`) so coverage work is started after `pytest-trio` is loaded.
The solution is to start the coverage before `pytest-trio` is loaded, so before pytest is called:
```
$ coverage -m pytest
```

`self._params` is a private structure used for the whole framework processing. I don't recommend using it to store custom variables. However, if you try to access to `request.refresh_token`, you should find the actual content of the `refresh_token` (list of parameters are listed in `class Request`, if available).

If you want to add custom data, you have at least two locations:
Either directly in `request`. Either in `request.client`. It will depend of the nature of your data. In the case you described, I will be more tempted to put stuff in `request.client`, this object can be a custom object, as long as you have an attribute `client_id` in it for the `oauthlib` processing.
